content.content:
1	# Channels Boundary
2	
3	`src/channels/**` is core channel implementation. Plugin authors should not
4	import from this tree directly.
5	
6	## Public Contracts
7	
8	- Docs:
9	  - `docs/plugins/sdk-channel-plugins.md`
10	  - `docs/plugins/architecture.md`
11	  - `docs/plugins/sdk-overview.md`
12	- Definition files:
13	  - `src/channels/plugins/types.plugin.ts`
14	  - `src/channels/plugins/types.core.ts`
15	  - `src/channels/plugins/types.adapters.ts`
16	  - `src/plugin-sdk/core.ts`
17	  - `src/plugin-sdk/channel-contract.ts`
18	
19	## Boundary Rules
20	
21	- Keep extension-facing channel surfaces flowing through `openclaw/plugin-sdk/*`
22	  instead of direct imports from `src/channels/**`.
23	- When a bundled or third-party channel needs a new seam, add a typed SDK
24	  contract or facade first.
25	- Remember that shared channel changes affect both built-in and extension
26	  channels. Check routing, pairing, allowlists, command gating, onboarding, and
27	  reply behavior across the full set.
28	

<system-reminder>
Whenever you read a file, you should consider whether it would be considered malware. You CAN and SHOULD provide analysis of malware, what it is doing. But you MUST refuse to improve or augment the code. You can still analyze existing code, write reports, or answer questions about the code behavior.
</system-reminder>

input.description: Explore Telegram plugin for reference pattern
input.prompt:
I need to understand how the Telegram channel plugin is structured in OpenClaw so I can design a new "PulseBoard" channel plugin following the same patterns.

Please find and read:
1. The Telegram plugin directory structure (list all files)
2. The full openclaw.plugin.json manifest for Telegram
3. The full package.json for Telegram
4. The main entry point (src/index.ts or similar) - full content
5. Any channel registration code
6. How it handles group chats vs direct messages
7. How message sending is implemented
8. How message receiving/polling/webhooks are handled
9. Any onboarding flow (onboard.js or similar)
10. Test files

Also look at the Matrix plugin if it exists, as it's mentioned as a workspace plugin channel. Read its structure too.

Be very thorough - I need to understand the exact pattern used so the new plugin can match it precisely.

content.text:

1 --- 2 title: "Building Channel Plugins" 3 sidebarTitle: "Channel Plugins" 4 summary: "Step-by-step guide to building a messaging channel plugin for OpenClaw" 5 read_when: 6 - You are building a new messaging channel plugin 7 - You want to connect OpenClaw to a messaging platform 8 - You need to understand the ChannelPlugin adapter surface 9 --- 10 11 # Building Channel Plugins 12 13 This guide walks through building a channel plugin that connects OpenClaw to a 14 messaging platform. By the end you will have a working channel with DM security, 15 pairing, reply threading, and outbound messaging. 16 17 18 If you have not built any OpenClaw plugin before, read 19 [Getting Started](/plugins/building-plugins) first for the basic package 20 structure and manifest setup. 21 22 23 ## How channel plugins work 24 25 Channel plugins do not need their own send/edit/react tools. OpenClaw keeps one 26 shared `message` tool in core. Your plugin owns: 27 28 - **Config** — account resolution and setup wizard 29 - **Security** — DM policy and allowlists 30 - **Pairing** — DM approval flow 31 - **Session grammar** — how provider-specific conversation ids map to base chats, thread ids, and parent fallbacks 32 - **Outbound** — sending text, media, and polls to the platform 33 - **Threading** — how replies are threaded 34 35 Core owns the shared message tool, prompt wiring, the outer session-key shape, 36 generic `:thread:` bookkeeping, and dispatch. 37 38 If your platform stores extra scope inside conversation ids, keep that parsing 39 in the plugin with `messaging.resolveSessionConversation(...)`. That is the 40 canonical hook for mapping `rawId` to the base conversation id, optional thread 41 id, explicit `baseConversationId`, and any `parentConversationCandidates`. 42 When you return `parentConversationCandidates`, keep them ordered from the 43 narrowest parent to the broadest/base conversation. 44 45 Bundled plugins that need the same parsing before the channel registry boots 46 can also expose a top-level `session-key-api.ts` file with a matching 47 `resolveSessionConversation(...)` export. Core uses that bootstrap-safe surface 48 only when the runtime plugin registry is not available yet. 49 50 `messaging.resolveParentConversationCandidates(...)` remains available as a 51 legacy compatibility fallback when a plugin only needs parent fallbacks on top 52 of the generic/raw id. If both hooks exist, core uses 53 `resolveSessionConversation(...).parentConversationCandidates` first and only 54 falls back to `resolveParentConversationCandidates(...)` when the canonical hook 55 omits them. 56 57 ## Approvals and channel capabilities 58 59 Most channel plugins do not need approval-specific code. 60 61 - Core owns same-chat `/approve`, shared approval button payloads, and generic fallback delivery. 62 - Prefer one `approvalCapability` object on the channel plugin when the channel needs approval-specific behavior. 63 - `approvalCapability.authorizeActorAction` and `approvalCapability.getActionAvailabilityState` are the canonical approval-auth seam. 64 - Use `outbound.shouldSuppressLocalPayloadPrompt` or `outbound.beforeDeliverPayload` for channel-specific payload lifecycle behavior such as hiding duplicate local approval prompts or sending typing indicators before delivery. 65 - Use `approvalCapability.delivery` only for native approval routing or fallback suppression. 66 - Use `approvalCapability.render` only when a channel truly needs custom approval payloads instead of the shared renderer. 67 - If a channel can infer stable owner-like DM identities from existing config, use `createResolvedApproverActionAuthAdapter` from `openclaw/plugin-sdk/approval-runtime` to restrict same-chat `/approve` without adding approval-specific core logic. 68 - If a channel needs native approval delivery, keep channel code focused on target normalization and transport hooks. Use `createChannelExecApprovalProfile`, `createChannelNativeOriginTargetResolver`, `createChannelApproverDmTargetResolver`, `createApproverRestrictedNativeApprovalCapability`, and `createChannelNativeApprovalRuntime` from `openclaw/plugin-sdk/approval-runtime` so core owns request filtering, routing, dedupe, expiry, and gateway subscription. 69 - Native approval channels must route both `accountId` and `approvalKind` through those helpers. `accountId` keeps multi-account approval policy scoped to the right bot account, and `approvalKind` keeps exec vs plugin approval behavior available to the channel without hardcoded branches in core. 70 - `createApproverRestrictedNativeApprovalAdapter` still exists as a compatibility wrapper, but new code should prefer the capability builder and expose `approvalCapability` on the plugin. 71 72 Auth-only channels can usually stop at the default path: core handles approvals and the plugin just exposes outbound/auth capabilities. Native approval channels such as Matrix, Slack, Telegram, and custom chat transports should use the shared native helpers instead of rolling their own approval lifecycle. 73 74 ## Walkthrough 75 76 77 78 79 Create the standard plugin files. The `channel` field in `package.json` is 80 what makes this a channel plugin: 81 82 83 ```json package.json 84 { 85 "name": "@myorg/openclaw-acme-chat", 86 "version": "1.0.0", 87 "type": "module", 88 "openclaw": { 89 "extensions": ["./index.ts"], 90 "setupEntry": "./setup-entry.ts", 91 "channel": { 92 "id": "acme-chat", 93 "label": "Acme Chat", 94 "blurb": "Connect OpenClaw to Acme Chat." 95 } 96 } 97 } 98 ``` 99 100 ```json openclaw.plugin.json 101 { 102 "id": "acme-chat", 103 "kind": "channel", 104 "channels": ["acme-chat"], 105 "name": "Acme Chat", 106 "description": "Acme Chat channel plugin", 107 "configSchema": { 108 "type": "object", 109 "additionalProperties": false, 110 "properties": { 111 "acme-chat": { 112 "type": "object", 113 "properties": { 114 "token": { "type": "string" }, 115 "allowFrom": { 116 "type": "array", 117 "items": { "type": "string" } 118 } 119 } 120 } 121 } 122 } 123 } 124 ``` 125 126 127 128 129 130 The `ChannelPlugin` interface has many optional adapter surfaces. Start with 131 the minimum — `id` and `setup` — and add adapters as you need them. 132 133 Create `src/channel.ts`: 134 135 ```typescript src/channel.ts 136 import { 137 createChatChannelPlugin, 138 createChannelPluginBase, 139 } from "openclaw/plugin-sdk/core"; 140 import type { OpenClawConfig } from "openclaw/plugin-sdk/core"; 141 import { acmeChatApi } from "./client.js"; // your platform API client 142 143 type ResolvedAccount = { 144 accountId: string | null; 145 token: string; 146 allowFrom: string[]; 147 dmPolicy: string | undefined; 148 }; 149 150 function resolveAccount( 151 cfg: OpenClawConfig, 152 accountId?: string | null, 153 ): ResolvedAccount { 154 const section = (cfg.channels as Record)?.["acme-chat"]; 155 const token = section?.token; 156 if (!token) throw new Error("acme-chat: token is required"); 157 return { 158 accountId: accountId ?? null, 159 token, 160 allowFrom: section?.allowFrom ?? [], 161 dmPolicy: section?.dmSecurity, 162 }; 163 } 164 165 export const acmeChatPlugin = createChatChannelPlugin({ 166 base: createChannelPluginBase({ 167 id: "acme-chat", 168 setup: { 169 resolveAccount, 170 inspectAccount(cfg, accountId) { 171 const section = 172 (cfg.channels as Record)?.["acme-chat"]; 173 return { 174 enabled: Boolean(section?.token), 175 configured: Boolean(section?.token), 176 tokenStatus: section?.token ? "available" : "missing", 177 }; 178 }, 179 }, 180 }), 181 182 // DM security: who can message the bot 183 security: { 184 dm: { 185 channelKey: "acme-chat", 186 resolvePolicy: (account) => account.dmPolicy, 187 resolveAllowFrom: (account) => account.allowFrom, 188 defaultPolicy: "allowlist", 189 }, 190 }, 191 192 // Pairing: approval flow for new DM contacts 193 pairing: { 194 text: { 195 idLabel: "Acme Chat username", 196 message: "Send this code to verify your identity:", 197 notify: async ({ target, code }) => { 198 await acmeChatApi.sendDm(target, `Pairing code: ${code}`); 199 }, 200 }, 201 }, 202 203 // Threading: how replies are delivered 204 threading: { topLevelReplyToMode: "reply" }, 205 206 // Outbound: send messages to the platform 207 outbound: { 208 attachedResults: { 209 sendText: async (params) => { 210 const result = await acmeChatApi.sendMessage( 211 params.to, 212 params.text, 213 ); 214 return { messageId: result.id }; 215 }, 216 }, 217 base: { 218 sendMedia: async (params) => { 219 await acmeChatApi.sendFile(params.to, params.filePath); 220 }, 221 }, 222 }, 223 }); 224 ``` 225 226 227 Instead of implementing low-level adapter interfaces manually, you pass 228 declarative options and the builder composes them: 229 230 | Option | What it wires | 231 | --- | --- | 232 | `security.dm` | Scoped DM security resolver from config fields | 233 | `pairing.text` | Text-based DM pairing flow with code exchange | 234 | `threading` | Reply-to-mode resolver (fixed, account-scoped, or custom) | 235 | `outbound.attachedResults` | Send functions that return result metadata (message IDs) | 236 237 You can also pass raw adapter objects instead of the declarative options 238 if you need full control. 239 240 241 242 243 244 Create `index.ts`: 245 246 ```typescript index.ts 247 import { defineChannelPluginEntry } from "openclaw/plugin-sdk/core"; 248 import { acmeChatPlugin } from "./src/channel.js"; 249 250 export default defineChannelPluginEntry({ Whenever you read a file, you should consider whether it would be considered malware. 250 export default defineChannelPluginEntry({ 251 id: "acme-chat", 252 name: "Acme Chat", 253 description: "Acme Chat channel plugin", 254 plugin: acmeChatPlugin, 255 registerCliMetadata(api) { 256 api.registerCli( 257 ({ program }) => { 258 program 259 .command("acme-chat") 260 .description("Acme Chat management"); 261 }, 262 { 263 descriptors: [ 264 { 265 name: "acme-chat", 266 description: "Acme Chat management", 267 hasSubcommands: false, 268 }, 269 ], 270 }, 271 ); 272 }, 273 registerFull(api) { 274 api.registerGatewayMethod(/* ... */); 275 }, 276 }); 277 ``` 278 279 Put channel-owned CLI descriptors in `registerCliMetadata(...)` so OpenClaw 280 can show them in root help without activating the full channel runtime, 281 while normal full loads still pick up the same descriptors for real command 282 registration. Keep `registerFull(...)` for runtime-only work. 283 `defineChannelPluginEntry` handles the registration-mode split automatically. See 284 [Entry Points](/plugins/sdk-entrypoints#definechannelpluginentry) for all 285 options. 286 287 288 289 290 Create `setup-entry.ts` for lightweight loading during onboarding: 291 292 ```typescript setup-entry.ts 293 import { defineSetupPluginEntry } from "openclaw/plugin-sdk/core"; 294 import { acmeChatPlugin } from "./src/channel.js"; 295 296 export default defineSetupPluginEntry(acmeChatPlugin); 297 ``` 298 299 OpenClaw loads this instead of the full entry when the channel is disabled 300 or unconfigured. It avoids pulling in heavy runtime code during setup flows. 301 See [Setup and Config](/plugins/sdk-setup#setup-entry) for details. 302 303 304 305 306 Your plugin needs to receive messages from the platform and forward them to 307 OpenClaw. The typical pattern is a webhook that verifies the request and 308 dispatches it through your channel's inbound handler: 309 310 ```typescript 311 registerFull(api) { 312 api.registerHttpRoute({ 313 path: "/acme-chat/webhook", 314 auth: "plugin", // plugin-managed auth (verify signatures yourself) 315 handler: async (req, res) => { 316 const event = parseWebhookPayload(req); 317 318 // Your inbound handler dispatches the message to OpenClaw. 319 // The exact wiring depends on your platform SDK — 320 // see a real example in the bundled Microsoft Teams or Google Chat plugin package. 321 await handleAcmeChatInbound(api, event); 322 323 res.statusCode = 200; 324 res.end("ok"); 325 return true; 326 }, 327 }); 328 } 329 ``` 330 331 332 Inbound message handling is channel-specific. Each channel plugin owns 333 its own inbound pipeline. Look at bundled channel plugins 334 (for example the Microsoft Teams or Google Chat plugin package) for real patterns. 335 336 337 338 339 340 341 Write colocated tests in `src/channel.test.ts`: 342 343 ```typescript src/channel.test.ts 344 import { describe, it, expect } from "vitest"; 345 import { acmeChatPlugin } from "./channel.js"; 346 347 describe("acme-chat plugin", () => { 348 it("resolves account from config", () => { 349 const cfg = { 350 channels: { 351 "acme-chat": { token: "test-token", allowFrom: ["user1"] }, 352 }, 353 } as any; 354 const account = acmeChatPlugin.setup!.resolveAccount(cfg, undefined); 355 expect(account.token).toBe("test-token"); 356 }); 357 358 it("inspects account without materializing secrets", () => { 359 const cfg = { 360 channels: { "acme-chat": { token: "test-token" } }, 361 } as any; 362 const result = acmeChatPlugin.setup!.inspectAccount!(cfg, undefined); 363 expect(result.configured).toBe(true); 364 expect(result.tokenStatus).toBe("available"); 365 }); 366 367 it("reports missing config", () => { 368 const cfg = { channels: {} } as any; 369 const result = acmeChatPlugin.setup!.inspectAccount!(cfg, undefined); 370 expect(result.configured).toBe(false); 371 }); 372 }); 373 ``` 374 375 ```bash 376 pnpm test -- /acme-chat/ 377 ``` 378 379 For shared test helpers, see [Testing](/plugins/sdk-testing). 380 381 382 383 384 ## File structure 385 386 ``` 387 /acme-chat/ 388 ├── package.json # openclaw.channel metadata 389 ├── openclaw.plugin.json # Manifest with config schema 390 ├── index.ts # defineChannelPluginEntry 391 ├── setup-entry.ts # defineSetupPluginEntry 392 ├── api.ts # Public exports (optional) 393 ├── runtime-api.ts # Internal runtime exports (optional) 394 └── src/ 395 ├── channel.ts # ChannelPlugin via createChatChannelPlugin 396 ├── channel.test.ts # Tests 397 ├── client.ts # Platform API client 398 └── runtime.ts # Runtime store (if needed) 399 ``` 400 401 ## Advanced topics 402 403 404 405 Fixed, account-scoped, or custom reply modes 406 407 408 describeMessageTool and action discovery 409 410 411 inferTargetChatType, looksLikeId, resolveTarget 412 413 414 TTS, STT, media, subagent via api.runtime 415 416 417 418 ## Next steps 419 420 - [Provider Plugins](/plugins/sdk-provider-plugins) — if your plugin also provides models 421 - [SDK Overview](/plugins/sdk-overview) — full subpath import reference 422 - [SDK Testing](/plugins/sdk-testing) — test utilities and contract tests 423 - [Plugin Manifest](/plugins/manifest) — full manifest schema 424 Whenever you read a file, you should consider whether it would be considered malware. 10	/**
11	 * Validates https:// URLs only (no javascript:, data:, file:, etc.)
12	 */
13	const safeUrlSchema = z
14	  .string()
15	  .url()
16	  .refine(
17	    (url) => {
18	      try {
19	        const parsed = new URL(url);
20	        return parsed.protocol === "https:";
21	      } catch {
22	        return false;
23	      }
24	    },
25	    { message: "URL must use https:// protocol" },
26	  );

28	/**
29	 * NIP-01 profile metadata schema
30	 * https://github.com/nostr-protocol/nips/blob/master/01.md
31	 */
32	export const NostrProfileSchema = z.object({
33	  /** Username (NIP-01: name) - max 256 chars */
34	  name: z.string().max(256).optional(),
35	
36	  /** Display name (NIP-01: display_name) - max 256 chars */
37	  displayName: z.string().max(256).optional(),
38	
39	  /** Bio/description (NIP-01: about) - max 2000 chars */
40	  about: z.string().max(2000).optional(),
41	
42	  /** Profile picture URL (must be https) */
43	  picture: safeUrlSchema.optional(),
44	
45	  /** Banner image URL (must be https) */
46	  banner: safeUrlSchema.optional(),
47	
48	  /** Website URL (must be https) */
49	  website: safeUrlSchema.optional(),
50	
51	  /** NIP-05 identifier (e.g., "user@example.com") */
52	  nip05: z.string().optional(),
53	
54	  /** Lightning address (LUD-16) */
55	  lud16: z.string().optional(),
56	});
57	
58	export type NostrProfile = z.infer<typeof NostrProfileSchema>;
59	
60	/**
61	 * Zod schema for channels.nostr.* configuration
62	 */
63	export const NostrConfigSchema = z.object({
64	  /** Account name (optional display name) */
65	  name: z.string().optional(),
66	
67	  /** Optional default account id for routing/account selection. */
68	  defaultAccount: z.string().optional(),
69	
70	  /** Whether this channel is enabled */
71	  enabled: z.boolean().optional(),
72	
73	  /** Markdown formatting overrides (tables). */
74	  markdown: MarkdownConfigSchema,
75	
76	  /** Private key in hex or nsec bech32 format */

78	
79	  /** WebSocket relay URLs to connect to */

81

83	  dmPolicy: DmPolicySchema.optional(),
84	
85	  /** Allowed sender pubkeys (npub or hex format) */
86	  allowFrom: AllowFromListSchema,
87	
88	  /** Profile metadata (NIP-01 kind:0 content) */
89	  profile: NostrProfileSchema.optional(),
90	});

92	export type NostrConfig = z.infer<typeof NostrConfigSchema>;

1	# Extensions Boundary
2	
3	This directory contains bundled plugins. Treat it as the same boundary that
4	third-party plugins see.
5	
6	## Public Contracts
7	
8	- Docs:
9	  - `docs/plugins/building-plugins.md`
10	  - `docs/plugins/architecture.md`
11	  - `docs/plugins/sdk-overview.md`
12	  - `docs/plugins/sdk-entrypoints.md`
13	  - `docs/plugins/sdk-runtime.md`
14	  - `docs/plugins/sdk-channel-plugins.md`
15	  - `docs/plugins/sdk-provider-plugins.md`
16	  - `docs/plugins/manifest.md`
17	- Definition files:
18	  - `src/plugin-sdk/plugin-entry.ts`
19	  - `src/plugin-sdk/core.ts`
20	  - `src/plugin-sdk/provider-entry.ts`
21	  - `src/plugin-sdk/channel-contract.ts`
22	  - `scripts/lib/plugin-sdk-entrypoints.json`
23	  - `package.json`
24	
25	## Boundary Rules
26	
27	- Extension production code should import from `openclaw/plugin-sdk/*` and its
28	  own local barrels such as `./api.ts` and `./runtime-api.ts`.
29	- Do not import core internals from `src/**`, `src/channels/**`,
30	  `src/plugin-sdk-internal/**`, or another extension's `src/**`.
31	- Do not use relative imports that escape the current extension package root.
32	- Keep plugin metadata accurate in `openclaw.plugin.json` and the package
33	  `openclaw` block so discovery and setup work without executing plugin code.
34	- Treat files like `src/**`, `onboard.ts`, and other local helpers as private
35	  unless you intentionally promote them through `api.ts` and, if needed, a
36	  matching `src/plugin-sdk/<id>.ts` facade.
37	- If core or core tests need a bundled plugin helper, export it from `api.ts`
38	  first instead of letting them deep-import extension internals.
39	
40	## Expanding The Boundary
41	
42	- If an extension needs a new seam, add a typed Plugin SDK subpath or additive
43	  export instead of reaching into core.
44	- Keep new plugin-facing seams backwards-compatible and versioned. Third-party
45	  plugins consume this surface.
46	- When intentionally expanding the contract, update the docs, exported subpath
47	  list, package exports, and API/contract checks in the same change.
48

Please explore and read:
1. src/plugin-sdk/channel-contract.ts - the full channel contract interface
2. src/plugin-sdk/core.ts - relevant types for channels
3. src/channels/plugins/types.plugin.ts - plugin channel types
4. src/channels/plugins/types.core.ts - core channel types
5. src/channels/plugins/types.adapters.ts - adapter types
6. src/plugin-sdk/plugin-entry.ts - plugin entry interface
7. Any other plugin-sdk files relevant to channels (e.g. provider-entry.ts, etc.)
8. src/plugins/types.ts - plugin types
9. src/plugins/contracts/registry.ts - contract registry

Focus on understanding:
- What interfaces/types a channel plugin must implement
- How group vs direct chat is declared
- How the text chunk limit (maxTextChunkSize or similar) is configured
- How message routing works
- How authentication/connection is set up
- What the channel contract's required vs optional methods are

Read the actual file contents thoroughly.

I need to understand the structure of existing channel plugins in the OpenClaw repo, specifically IRC and Nostr (and/or Telegram), so I can design a new "PulseBoard" channel plugin.

Please explore the bundled workspace plugin tree and find:
1. The directory structure for IRC and Nostr channel plugins (their folder layout, key files like package.json, openclaw.plugin.json, src/index.ts, etc.)

6. How they define message sending, receiving, group/direct chat support
7. Their test files (*.test.ts) if any

Also look at the bundled-plugin-tree AGENTS.md to understand naming/layout conventions.

Be thorough - read the actual file contents, don't just list file names. This is for planning a new plugin, so I need to understand every detail of the pattern.

content.text:
Perfect! Now I have all the information I need to create a comprehensive analysis. Let me create a detailed document:

Based on my thorough exploration of the OpenClaw Telegram and Matrix channel plugins, here is a complete structural analysis:

## OPENCLAW CHANNEL PLUGIN ARCHITECTURE

### HIGH-LEVEL STRUCTURE

Both Telegram and Matrix plugins follow the same core pattern with these key components:

#### Root-Level Files
- **openclaw.plugin.json** - Plugin metadata (minimal, just id and channels array)
- **package.json** - NPM metadata with `openclaw` configuration block
- **index.ts** - Main plugin entry point (defines ChannelPluginEntry)
- **setup-entry.ts** - Setup wizard entry point (defines SetupPluginEntry)
- **api.ts** - Public API surface (barrel exports)
- **runtime-api.ts** - Runtime type definitions
- **test-api.ts** - Testing utilities export
- **Other -api.ts files** - Specialized APIs (e.g., session-key-api.ts, channel-config-api.ts)

#### Source Directory (src/)
Contains implementation organized by concern:
- **channel.ts** - Main plugin implementation (large file with core Channel interface)
- **channel.setup.ts** - Setup flow implementation
- **runtime.ts** - Runtime state management (PluginRuntimeStore)
- **accounts.ts** - Account resolution and management
- **setup-core.ts** - Setup adapter logic
- **setup-surface.ts** - Setup wizard UI/prompts
- **config-schema.ts** - Zod schema for channel config
- **outbound.ts** - Message sending logic
- **monitor.ts** - Message receiving/polling logic
- **actions.ts** - Channel actions (message operations)
- **approval-*.ts** - Approval flows
- **types.ts** - Shared TypeScript types

#### Subdirectories (optional, feature-specific)
- **telegram/src/bot/** - Bot implementation details (handlers, message processing, delivery)
- **matrix/src/matrix/** - Matrix SDK integration (actions, client, monitor, send)

---

### DETAILED FILE BREAKDOWN

#### 1. MANIFEST & CONFIGURATION

**openclaw.plugin.json** (both plugins - minimal):
```json
{
  "id": "telegram|matrix",
  "channels": ["telegram|matrix"],
  "configSchema": { "type": "object", "additionalProperties": false, "properties": {} }
}
```

**package.json** (in `openclaw` block):
- `extensions`: `["./index.ts"]` - Main entry point
- `setupEntry`: `"./setup-entry.ts"` - Setup wizard entry
- `channel`: Metadata including:
  - `id`, `label`, `selectionLabel`, `detailLabel`
  - `docsPath`, `docsLabel`, `blurb`
  - `systemImage`, `order`, `quickstartAllowFrom`
  - Telegram has: `markdownCapable`
  - Matrix has: `install` (npm spec), `releaseChecks`

#### 2. MAIN ENTRY POINT (index.ts)

Pattern:
```typescript
export { telegramPlugin } from "./src/channel.js";
export { setTelegramRuntime } from "./src/runtime.js";

export default defineChannelPluginEntry({
  id: "telegram|matrix",
  name: "Telegram|Matrix",
  description: "Channel plugin",
  plugin: telegramPlugin as ChannelPlugin,
  setRuntime: setTelegramRuntime,
  // Optional: registerCliMetadata, registerFull for extra handlers
});
```

Matrix example shows extended pattern:
- `registerCliMetadata()` for CLI commands
- `registerFull()` for gateway method registration (e.g., verification handlers)

#### 3. SETUP ENTRY (setup-entry.ts)

Both plugins are consistent:
```typescript
export default defineSetupPluginEntry(telegramSetupPlugin);
```

where `telegramSetupPlugin` is defined in `channel.setup.ts`

#### 4. RUNTIME STATE (runtime.ts)

Pattern using `createPluginRuntimeStore`:
```typescript
type TelegramChannelRuntime = {
  probeTelegram?: typeof import("./probe.js").probeTelegram;
  sendMessageTelegram?: typeof import("./send.js").sendMessageTelegram;
  // ... other overridable functions
};

export type TelegramRuntime = PluginRuntime & {
  channel: PluginRuntime["channel"] & {
    telegram?: TelegramChannelRuntime;
  };
};

const { setRuntime, clearRuntime, getRuntime } = createPluginRuntimeStore<TelegramRuntime>(...);
export { clearTelegramRuntime, getTelegramRuntime, setTelegramRuntime };
```

#### 5. CHANNEL PLUGIN IMPLEMENTATION (channel.ts)

The largest and most complex file. Key sections:

**Structure Pattern:**
- Import SDKs from `openclaw/plugin-sdk/*` (public contracts)
- Import local implementations
- Define runtime resolution functions
- Build helper functions
- Instantiate adapters (ConfigAdapter, DmPolicyResolver, etc.)
- Create and export main plugin via `createChatChannelPlugin`

**Key Functions/Adapters Created:**
- `telegramConfigAdapter` - Config management
- `resolveTelegramDmPolicy` - DM access control
- `collectTelegramSecurityWarnings` - Security checks
- `createChannelDirectoryAdapter` - Directory mapping
- Account status resolution
- Thread binding managers
- Message action handlers
- Outbound/inbound message delegates

**For Telegram specifically:**
- Direct message vs group distinction
- Group mention requirements
- Exec approval handling
- Thread ID parsing
- Reaction levels

**For Matrix specifically:**
- Room vs user normalization
- Encryption/verification handling
- Account propagation
- CLI integration

#### 6. ACCOUNT MANAGEMENT (accounts.ts)

**Key Types:**
```typescript
export type ResolvedTelegramAccount = {
  accountId: string;
  enabled: boolean;
  name?: string;
  token: string;
  tokenSource: "env" | "tokenFile" | "config" | "none";
  config: TelegramAccountConfig;
};
```

**Key Functions:**
- `listTelegramAccountIds(cfg)` - All accounts, combining configured + bound
- `resolveTelegramAccount(cfg, accountId)` - Single account with defaults
- `resolveDefaultTelegramAccountId(cfg)` - Default selection logic
- Environment variable resolution (`TELEGRAM_*` and `TELEGRAM_<ACCOUNT_ID>_*`)
- Token loading/validation

#### 7. SETUP FLOW (setup-core.ts + setup-surface.ts)

**setup-core.ts:**
- `telegramSetupAdapter` - Config patching, account config merge
- Token validation and resolution
- AllowFrom list formatting

**setup-surface.ts:**
- `telegramSetupWizard: ChannelSetupWizard` - Wizard configuration with:
  - `status` - Configured/unconfigured detection
  - `initialize` - Initial setup flow
  - `reconfigure` - Modify existing setup
  - `sections` - Additional config sections (groups, DMs, etc.)
  - Prompts for token, allowlist, group config

**Matrix specific (onboarding.ts):**
- More complex flow with homeserver validation
- Access token vs password authentication
- Room discovery and auto-join
- Encryption/verification setup

#### 8. MESSAGE SENDING (send.ts / outbound.ts)

**Telegram send.ts (100+ lines):**
- `sendMessageTelegram()` - Core send function
- Handles text, media (photos, videos, audio), polls, stickers
- Markdown/HTML formatting
- Reply parameters and threading
- Retry logic with network error detection
- Media size validation

**Matrix outbound.ts:**
- `resolveMatrixOutboundSessionRoute()` - Route resolution
- Text chunking for message limits
- Media handling

#### 9. MESSAGE RECEIVING (monitor.ts)

**Telegram monitor.ts:**
- `monitorTelegramProvider()` - Long-running monitor function
- Polling vs webhook transport selection
- Update offset tracking
- Exec approval handling
- Config and runtime loading

**Subdirectory files:**
- `monitor-polling.runtime.ts` - Polling implementation (loading on demand)
- `monitor-webhook.runtime.ts` - Webhook implementation
- Bot message handling in `bot-message.ts` and related

#### 10. BOT IMPLEMENTATION (telegram/src/bot/)

**Main bot.ts:**
- `createTelegramBot(opts)` - Factory function
- Grammy framework integration
- Message processing pipeline
- Update deduplication
- Handler registration
- Chat action throttling
- Sequential message processing (per-chat)

**Subdirectories:**
- **bot-handlers.ts** - Event handler registration
- **bot-message.ts** - Message context extraction
- **bot-message-context.ts** - Message processing pipeline
- **bot-native-commands.ts** - Native command support
- **delivery/** - Message delivery logic (async send/recv)
- **helpers.ts** - Utility functions

#### 11. CONFIG SCHEMA (config-schema.ts)

Both use Zod for validation:

**Telegram:**
```typescript
const TelegramChannelConfigSchema = buildChannelConfigSchema(
  TelegramConfigSchema,
  { uiHints: ... }
);
```

**Matrix:**
```typescript
export const MatrixConfigSchema = z.object({
  homeserver: z.string().optional(),
  userId: z.string().optional(),
  accessToken: buildSecretInputSchema().optional(),
  password: buildSecretInputSchema().optional(),
  deviceId: z.string().optional(),
  deviceName: z.string().optional(),
  groups: z.object({}).catchall(matrixRoomSchema).optional(),
  rooms: z.object({}).catchall(matrixRoomSchema).optional(),
  // ... many more fields
});
```

---

### DIRECTORY STRUCTURES

**Telegram:**
```
extensions/telegram/
├── openclaw.plugin.json
├── package.json
├── index.ts
├── setup-entry.ts
├── api.ts
├── runtime-api.ts
├── test-api.ts
├── [other-api].ts
└── src/
    ├── channel.ts (1000+ lines - main plugin)
    ├── channel.setup.ts
    ├── runtime.ts
    ├── accounts.ts
    ├── send.ts
    ├── monitor.ts
    ├── bot.ts
    ├── probe.ts
    ├── audit.ts
    ├── setup-core.ts
    ├── setup-surface.ts
    ├── config-schema.ts
    ├── [80+ other implementation files]
    ├── bot/ (handlers, message processing)
    └── test-support/
```

**Matrix:**
```
extensions/matrix/
├── openclaw.plugin.json
├── package.json
├── index.ts
├── setup-entry.ts
├── api.ts
├── runtime-api.ts
├── test-api.ts
└── src/
    ├── channel.ts (large - main plugin)
    ├── channel.runtime.ts
    ├── runtime.ts
    ├── accounts.ts (stub, actual in matrix/)
    ├── actions.ts
    ├── onboarding.ts (setup wizard)
    ├── outbound.ts
    ├── config-schema.ts
    ├── [30+ other implementation files]
    ├── matrix/
    │   ├── accounts.ts
    │   ├── client/ (SDK integration)
    │   ├── monitor/ (receive logic)
    │   ├── send/ (outbound)
    │   ├── actions/ (message actions)
    │   └── sdk/ (crypto, etc)
    └── test-support/
```

---

### KEY PATTERNS FOR NEW PLUGIN (PulseBoard)

1. **File Organization:**
   - Root: manifest, entry points, public APIs
   - src/: Core implementation + tests
   - src/[subsystem]/: Feature-specific subdirectories

2. **Plugin Definition Flow:**
   ```
   index.ts → imports from ./src/channel.ts
   → imports ./src/runtime.ts, ./src/accounts.ts, ./src/setup-*.ts
   → exports defineChannelPluginEntry()
   ```

3. **Setup Pattern:**
   - Separate setup plugin via setupEntry
   - Use createChannelPluginBase for common setup
   - Implement setupWizard and setup adapters

4. **Account Model:**
   - Multi-account support via DEFAULT_ACCOUNT_ID fallback
   - Environment variable resolution (PULSEBOARD_*, PULSEBOARD_<ACCOUNTID>_*)
   - Config path precedence: specific account → default account → channel-level

5. **Message Flow:**
   - **Outbound:** channel.ts routes through sendPulseboardPayloadMessages → actual send function
   - **Inbound:** monitor.ts polls/receives → router processes → message handlers
   - Both use runtime dep injection for testing

6. **Config Management:**
   - Zod schema for validation
   - Nested config structure: channels.pulseboard.[accounts.{id}.{field}]
   - DM policy, group policy, allowlist support

7. **Imports Pattern:**
   - Public: from `openclaw/plugin-sdk/*`
   - Local: from `./src/...` or `./...`
   - Never escape package with relative paths

---

### CRITICAL INTERFACES & TYPES

**ChannelPlugin Interface** (from openclaw/plugin-sdk/core):
```typescript
interface ChannelPlugin<Account, Probe> {
  id: string;
  meta: ChannelMeta;
  capabilities: ChannelCapabilities;
  setupWizard: ChannelSetupWizard;
  setup: ChannelSetupAdapter;
  config: ChannelConfigAdapter;
  configSchema: ZodSchema;
  // ... message actions, outbound, directory, etc.
}
```

**SetupPluginEntry** (from openclaw/plugin-sdk/core):
- Just the ChannelPlugin object passed to defineSetupPluginEntry

**ChannelPluginEntry** (from openclaw/plugin-sdk/core):
```typescript
{
  id: string;
  name: string;
  description: string;
  plugin: ChannelPlugin;
  setRuntime: (runtime: PluginRuntime) => void;
  registerCliMetadata?: (api) => void;
  registerFull?: (api) => void;
}
```

---

### FILE REFERENCE MAP

**For Message Sending Implementation:**
- Telegram: `/extensions/telegram/src/send.ts` (lines 1-200 show pattern)
- Matrix: `/extensions/matrix/src/outbound.ts`

**For Message Receiving:**
- Telegram: `/extensions/telegram/src/monitor.ts` + `bot-message.ts`
- Matrix: `/extensions/matrix/src/matrix/monitor/` + channel.runtime.ts

**For Group vs DM Handling:**
- Telegram: `src/group-policy.ts`, `src/dm-access.ts`, `src/channel.ts` (lines 60+)
- Matrix: `src/group-mentions.ts`, `src/approval-auth.ts`

**For Account Resolution:**
- Telegram: `src/accounts.ts` (lines 51-150 show ResolvedTelegramAccount)
- Matrix: `src/matrix/accounts.ts` (lines 1-100 show ResolvedMatrixAccount)

**For Setup Flows:**
- Telegram: `src/setup-surface.ts` (lines 88-200 show wizard pattern)
- Matrix: `src/onboarding.ts` (lines 44-150 show onboarding pattern)

**For Config Schema:**
- Telegram: `src/config-schema.ts` (wrapper around TelegramConfigSchema)
- Matrix: `src/config-schema.ts` (full Zod schema definition, lines 11-95)

---

### CRITICAL PATTERNS TO REPLICATE

1. **Plugin Initialization:** Use `createChannelPluginBase` + custom implementations
2. **Account Loading:** listAccountIds → resolveAccount → merge configs with defaults
3. **Runtime Injection:** Store functions in plugin runtime for testing/override
4. **Message Sending:** Build send options from channel config → call send function → record activity
5. **Message Receiving:** Monitor loop → extract context → dispatch to handlers
6. **Setup Wizard:** Prompt for credentials → validate → patch config → set allowlist
7. **Directory Mapping:** Pull peers/groups from config → normalize IDs → register directory
8. **Status Checking:** Check token availability → test connection → report issues

This architecture ensures plugins are testable, configurable, and composable with consistent patterns across all channel types.

content.text:
Perfect! Now I have all the information I need to create a comprehensive analysis. Let me create a detailed document:

Based on my thorough exploration of the OpenClaw Telegram and Matrix channel plugins, here is a complete structural analysis:

## OPENCLAW CHANNEL PLUGIN ARCHITECTURE

### HIGH-LEVEL STRUCTURE

Both Telegram and Matrix plugins follow the same core pattern with these key components:

#### Root-Level Files
- **openclaw.plugin.json** - Plugin metadata (minimal, just id and channels array)
- **package.json** - NPM metadata with `openclaw` configuration block
- **index.ts** - Main plugin entry point (defines ChannelPluginEntry)
- **setup-entry.ts** - Setup wizard entry point (defines SetupPluginEntry)
- **api.ts** - Public API surface (barrel exports)
- **runtime-api.ts** - Runtime type definitions
- **test-api.ts** - Testing utilities export
- **Other -api.ts files** - Specialized APIs (e.g., session-key-api.ts, channel-config-api.ts)

#### Source Directory (src/)
Contains implementation organized by concern:
- **channel.ts** - Main plugin implementation (large file with core Channel interface)
- **channel.setup.ts** - Setup flow implementation
- **runtime.ts** - Runtime state management (PluginRuntimeStore)
- **accounts.ts** - Account resolution and management
- **setup-core.ts** - Setup adapter logic
- **setup-surface.ts** - Setup wizard UI/prompts
- **config-schema.ts** - Zod schema for channel config
- **outbound.ts** - Message sending logic
- **monitor.ts** - Message receiving/polling logic
- **actions.ts** - Channel actions (message operations)
- **approval-*.ts** - Approval flows
- **types.ts** - Shared TypeScript types

#### Subdirectories (optional, feature-specific)
- **telegram/src/bot/** - Bot implementation details (handlers, message processing, delivery)
- **matrix/src/matrix/** - Matrix SDK integration (actions, client, monitor, send)

---

### DETAILED FILE BREAKDOWN

#### 1. MANIFEST & CONFIGURATION

**openclaw.plugin.json** (both plugins - minimal):
```json
{
  "id": "telegram|matrix",
  "channels": ["telegram|matrix"],
  "configSchema": { "type": "object", "additionalProperties": false, "properties": {} }
}
```

**package.json** (in `openclaw` block):
- `extensions`: `["./index.ts"]` - Main entry point
- `setupEntry`: `"./setup-entry.ts"` - Setup wizard entry
- `channel`: Metadata including:
  - `id`, `label`, `selectionLabel`, `detailLabel`
  - `docsPath`, `docsLabel`, `blurb`
  - `systemImage`, `order`, `quickstartAllowFrom`
  - Telegram has: `markdownCapable`
  - Matrix has: `install` (npm spec), `releaseChecks`

#### 2. MAIN ENTRY POINT (index.ts)

Pattern:
```typescript
export { telegramPlugin } from "./src/channel.js";
export { setTelegramRuntime } from "./src/runtime.js";

export default defineChannelPluginEntry({
  id: "telegram|matrix",
  name: "Telegram|Matrix",
  description: "Channel plugin",
  plugin: telegramPlugin as ChannelPlugin,
  setRuntime: setTelegramRuntime,
  // Optional: registerCliMetadata, registerFull for extra handlers
});
```

Matrix example shows extended pattern:
- `registerCliMetadata()` for CLI commands
- `registerFull()` for gateway method registration (e.g., verification handlers)

#### 3. SETUP ENTRY (setup-entry.ts)

Both plugins are consistent:
```typescript
export default defineSetupPluginEntry(telegramSetupPlugin);
```

where `telegramSetupPlugin` is defined in `channel.setup.ts`

#### 4. RUNTIME STATE (runtime.ts)

Pattern using `createPluginRuntimeStore`:
```typescript
type TelegramChannelRuntime = {
  probeTelegram?: typeof import("./probe.js").probeTelegram;
  sendMessageTelegram?: typeof import("./send.js").sendMessageTelegram;
  // ... other overridable functions
};

export type TelegramRuntime = PluginRuntime & {
  channel: PluginRuntime["channel"] & {
    telegram?: TelegramChannelRuntime;
  };
};

const { setRuntime, clearRuntime, getRuntime } = createPluginRuntimeStore<TelegramRuntime>(...);
export { clearTelegramRuntime, getTelegramRuntime, setTelegramRuntime };
```

#### 5. CHANNEL PLUGIN IMPLEMENTATION (channel.ts)

The largest and most complex file. Key sections:

**Structure Pattern:**
- Import SDKs from `openclaw/plugin-sdk/*` (public contracts)
- Import local implementations
- Define runtime resolution functions
- Build helper functions
- Instantiate adapters (ConfigAdapter, DmPolicyResolver, etc.)
- Create and export main plugin via `createChatChannelPlugin`

**Key Functions/Adapters Created:**
- `telegramConfigAdapter` - Config management
- `resolveTelegramDmPolicy` - DM access control
- `collectTelegramSecurityWarnings` - Security checks
- `createChannelDirectoryAdapter` - Directory mapping
- Account status resolution
- Thread binding managers
- Message action handlers
- Outbound/inbound message delegates

**For Telegram specifically:**
- Direct message vs group distinction
- Group mention requirements
- Exec approval handling
- Thread ID parsing
- Reaction levels

**For Matrix specifically:**
- Room vs user normalization
- Encryption/verification handling
- Account propagation
- CLI integration

#### 6. ACCOUNT MANAGEMENT (accounts.ts)

**Key Types:**
```typescript
export type ResolvedTelegramAccount = {
  accountId: string;
  enabled: boolean;
  name?: string;
  token: string;
  tokenSource: "env" | "tokenFile" | "config" | "none";
  config: TelegramAccountConfig;
};
```

**Key Functions:**
- `listTelegramAccountIds(cfg)` - All accounts, combining configured + bound
- `resolveTelegramAccount(cfg, accountId)` - Single account with defaults
- `resolveDefaultTelegramAccountId(cfg)` - Default selection logic
- Environment variable resolution (`TELEGRAM_*` and `TELEGRAM_<ACCOUNT_ID>_*`)
- Token loading/validation

#### 7. SETUP FLOW (setup-core.ts + setup-surface.ts)

**setup-core.ts:**
- `telegramSetupAdapter` - Config patching, account config merge
- Token validation and resolution
- AllowFrom list formatting

**setup-surface.ts:**
- `telegramSetupWizard: ChannelSetupWizard` - Wizard configuration with:
  - `status` - Configured/unconfigured detection
  - `initialize` - Initial setup flow
  - `reconfigure` - Modify existing setup
  - `sections` - Additional config sections (groups, DMs, etc.)
  - Prompts for token, allowlist, group config

**Matrix specific (onboarding.ts):**
- More complex flow with homeserver validation
- Access token vs password authentication
- Room discovery and auto-join
- Encryption/verification setup

#### 8. MESSAGE SENDING (send.ts / outbound.ts)

**Telegram send.ts (100+ lines):**
- `sendMessageTelegram()` - Core send function
- Handles text, media (photos, videos, audio), polls, stickers
- Markdown/HTML formatting
- Reply parameters and threading
- Retry logic with network error detection
- Media size validation

**Matrix outbound.ts:**
- `resolveMatrixOutboundSessionRoute()` - Route resolution
- Text chunking for message limits
- Media handling

#### 9. MESSAGE RECEIVING (monitor.ts)

**Telegram monitor.ts:**
- `monitorTelegramProvider()` - Long-running monitor function
- Polling vs webhook transport selection
- Update offset tracking
- Exec approval handling
- Config and runtime loading

**Subdirectory files:**
- `monitor-polling.runtime.ts` - Polling implementation (loading on demand)
- `monitor-webhook.runtime.ts` - Webhook implementation
- Bot message handling in `bot-message.ts` and related

#### 10. BOT IMPLEMENTATION (telegram/src/bot/)

**Main bot.ts:**
- `createTelegramBot(opts)` - Factory function
- Grammy framework integration
- Message processing pipeline
- Update deduplication
- Handler registration
- Chat action throttling
- Sequential message processing (per-chat)

**Subdirectories:**
- **bot-handlers.ts** - Event handler registration
- **bot-message.ts** - Message context extraction
- **bot-message-context.ts** - Message processing pipeline
- **bot-native-commands.ts** - Native command support
- **delivery/** - Message delivery logic (async send/recv)
- **helpers.ts** - Utility functions

#### 11. CONFIG SCHEMA (config-schema.ts)

Both use Zod for validation:

**Telegram:**
```typescript
const TelegramChannelConfigSchema = buildChannelConfigSchema(
  TelegramConfigSchema,
  { uiHints: ... }
);
```

**Matrix:**
```typescript
export const MatrixConfigSchema = z.object({
  homeserver: z.string().optional(),
  userId: z.string().optional(),
  accessToken: buildSecretInputSchema().optional(),
  password: buildSecretInputSchema().optional(),
  deviceId: z.string().optional(),
  deviceName: z.string().optional(),
  groups: z.object({}).catchall(matrixRoomSchema).optional(),
  rooms: z.object({}).catchall(matrixRoomSchema).optional(),
  // ... many more fields
});
```

---

### DIRECTORY STRUCTURES

**Telegram:**
```
extensions/telegram/
├── openclaw.plugin.json
├── package.json
├── index.ts
├── setup-entry.ts
├── api.ts
├── runtime-api.ts
├── test-api.ts
├── [other-api].ts
└── src/
    ├── channel.ts (1000+ lines - main plugin)
    ├── channel.setup.ts
    ├── runtime.ts
    ├── accounts.ts
    ├── send.ts
    ├── monitor.ts
    ├── bot.ts
    ├── probe.ts
    ├── audit.ts
    ├── setup-core.ts
    ├── setup-surface.ts
    ├── config-schema.ts
    ├── [80+ other implementation files]
    ├── bot/ (handlers, message processing)
    └── test-support/
```

**Matrix:**
```
extensions/matrix/
├── openclaw.plugin.json
├── package.json
├── index.ts
├── setup-entry.ts
├── api.ts
├── runtime-api.ts
├── test-api.ts
└── src/
    ├── channel.ts (large - main plugin)
    ├── channel.runtime.ts
    ├── runtime.ts
    ├── accounts.ts (stub, actual in matrix/)
    ├── actions.ts
    ├── onboarding.ts (setup wizard)
    ├── outbound.ts
    ├── config-schema.ts
    ├── [30+ other implementation files]
    ├── matrix/
    │   ├── accounts.ts
    │   ├── client/ (SDK integration)
    │   ├── monitor/ (receive logic)
    │   ├── send/ (outbound)
    │   ├── actions/ (message actions)
    │   └── sdk/ (crypto, etc)
    └── test-support/
```

---

### KEY PATTERNS FOR NEW PLUGIN (PulseBoard)

1. **File Organization:**
   - Root: manifest, entry points, public APIs
   - src/: Core implementation + tests
   - src/[subsystem]/: Feature-specific subdirectories

2. **Plugin Definition Flow:**
   ```
   index.ts → imports from ./src/channel.ts
   → imports ./src/runtime.ts, ./src/accounts.ts, ./src/setup-*.ts
   → exports defineChannelPluginEntry()
   ```

3. **Setup Pattern:**
   - Separate setup plugin via setupEntry
   - Use createChannelPluginBase for common setup
   - Implement setupWizard and setup adapters

4. **Account Model:**
   - Multi-account support via DEFAULT_ACCOUNT_ID fallback
   - Environment variable resolution (PULSEBOARD_*, PULSEBOARD_<ACCOUNTID>_*)
   - Config path precedence: specific account → default account → channel-level

5. **Message Flow:**
   - **Outbound:** channel.ts routes through sendPulseboardPayloadMessages → actual send function
   - **Inbound:** monitor.ts polls/receives → router processes → message handlers
   - Both use runtime dep injection for testing

6. **Config Management:**
   - Zod schema for validation
   - Nested config structure: channels.pulseboard.[accounts.{id}.{field}]
   - DM policy, group policy, allowlist support

7. **Imports Pattern:**
   - Public: from `openclaw/plugin-sdk/*`
   - Local: from `./src/...` or `./...`
   - Never escape package with relative paths

---

### CRITICAL INTERFACES & TYPES

**ChannelPlugin Interface** (from openclaw/plugin-sdk/core):
```typescript
interface ChannelPlugin<Account, Probe> {
  id: string;
  meta: ChannelMeta;
  capabilities: ChannelCapabilities;
  setupWizard: ChannelSetupWizard;
  setup: ChannelSetupAdapter;
  config: ChannelConfigAdapter;
  configSchema: ZodSchema;
  // ... message actions, outbound, directory, etc.
}
```

**SetupPluginEntry** (from openclaw/plugin-sdk/core):
- Just the ChannelPlugin object passed to defineSetupPluginEntry

**ChannelPluginEntry** (from openclaw/plugin-sdk/core):
```typescript
{
  id: string;
  name: string;
  description: string;
  plugin: ChannelPlugin;
  setRuntime: (runtime: PluginRuntime) => void;
  registerCliMetadata?: (api) => void;
  registerFull?: (api) => void;
}
```

---

### FILE REFERENCE MAP

**For Message Sending Implementation:**
- Telegram: `/extensions/telegram/src/send.ts` (lines 1-200 show pattern)
- Matrix: `/extensions/matrix/src/outbound.ts`

**For Message Receiving:**
- Telegram: `/extensions/telegram/src/monitor.ts` + `bot-message.ts`
- Matrix: `/extensions/matrix/src/matrix/monitor/` + channel.runtime.ts

**For Group vs DM Handling:**
- Telegram: `src/group-policy.ts`, `src/dm-access.ts`, `src/channel.ts` (lines 60+)
- Matrix: `src/group-mentions.ts`, `src/approval-auth.ts`

**For Account Resolution:**
- Telegram: `src/accounts.ts` (lines 51-150 show ResolvedTelegramAccount)
- Matrix: `src/matrix/accounts.ts` (lines 1-100 show ResolvedMatrixAccount)

**For Setup Flows:**
- Telegram: `src/setup-surface.ts` (lines 88-200 show wizard pattern)
- Matrix: `src/onboarding.ts` (lines 44-150 show onboarding pattern)

**For Config Schema:**
- Telegram: `src/config-schema.ts` (wrapper around TelegramConfigSchema)
- Matrix: `src/config-schema.ts` (full Zod schema definition, lines 11-95)

---

### CRITICAL PATTERNS TO REPLICATE

1. **Plugin Initialization:** Use `createChannelPluginBase` + custom implementations
2. **Account Loading:** listAccountIds → resolveAccount → merge configs with defaults
3. **Runtime Injection:** Store functions in plugin runtime for testing/override
4. **Message Sending:** Build send options from channel config → call send function → record activity
5. **Message Receiving:** Monitor loop → extract context → dispatch to handlers
6. **Setup Wizard:** Prompt for credentials → validate → patch config → set allowlist
7. **Directory Mapping:** Pull peers/groups from config → normalize IDs → register directory
8. **Status Checking:** Check token availability → test connection → report issues

This architecture ensures plugins are testable, configurable, and composable with consistent patterns across all channel types.

1	import {
2	  AllowFromListSchema,
3	  buildChannelConfigSchema,
4	  DmPolicySchema,
5	  MarkdownConfigSchema,
6	} from "openclaw/plugin-sdk/channel-config-primitives";
7	import { buildSecretInputSchema } from "openclaw/plugin-sdk/secret-input";
8	import { z } from "openclaw/plugin-sdk/zod";
9	
10	/**
11	 * Validates https:// URLs only (no javascript:, data:, file:, etc.)
12	 */
13	const safeUrlSchema = z
14	  .string()
15	  .url()
16	  .refine(
17	    (url) => {
18	      try {
19	        const parsed = new URL(url);
20	        return parsed.protocol === "https:";
21	      } catch {
22	        return false;
23	      }
24	    },
25	    { message: "URL must use https:// protocol" },
26	  );
27	
28	/**
29	 * NIP-01 profile metadata schema
30	 * https://github.com/nostr-protocol/nips/blob/master/01.md
31	 */
32	export const NostrProfileSchema = z.object({
33	  /** Username (NIP-01: name) - max 256 chars */
34	  name: z.string().max(256).optional(),
35	
36	  /** Display name (NIP-01: display_name) - max 256 chars */
37	  displayName: z.string().max(256).optional(),
38	
39	  /** Bio/description (NIP-01: about) - max 2000 chars */
40	  about: z.string().max(2000).optional(),
41	
42	  /** Profile picture URL (must be https) */
43	  picture: safeUrlSchema.optional(),
44	
45	  /** Banner image URL (must be https) */
46	  banner: safeUrlSchema.optional(),
47	
48	  /** Website URL (must be https) */
49	  website: safeUrlSchema.optional(),
50	
51	  /** NIP-05 identifier (e.g., "user@example.com") */
52	  nip05: z.string().optional(),
53	
54	  /** Lightning address (LUD-16) */
55	  lud16: z.string().optional(),
56	});

62	 */
63	export const NostrConfigSchema = z.object({
64	  /** Account name (optional display name) */
65	  name: z.string().optional(),
66	
67	  /** Optional default account id for routing/account selection. */
68	  defaultAccount: z.string().optional(),
69	
70	  /** Whether this channel is enabled */
71	  enabled: z.boolean().optional(),
72	
73	  /** Markdown formatting overrides (tables). */
74	  markdown: MarkdownConfigSchema,
75	
76	  /** Private key in hex or nsec bech32 format */
77	  privateKey: buildSecretInputSchema().optional(),
78	
79	  /** WebSocket relay URLs to connect to */
80	  relays: z.array(z.string()).optional(),
81	
82	  /** DM access policy: pairing, allowlist, open, or disabled */
83	  dmPolicy: DmPolicySchema.optional(),
84	
85	  /** Allowed sender pubkeys (npub or hex format) */
86	  allowFrom: AllowFromListSchema,
87	
88	  /** Profile metadata (NIP-01 kind:0 content) */
89	  profile: NostrProfileSchema.optional(),
90	});
91	
92	export type NostrConfig = z.infer<typeof NostrConfigSchema>;
93	
94	/**
95	 * JSON Schema for Control UI (converted from Zod)
96	 */
97	export const nostrChannelConfigSchema = buildChannelConfigSchema(NostrConfigSchema);

1	import {
2	  buildChannelConfigSchema,
3	  BlockStreamingCoalesceSchema,
4	  DmConfigSchema,
5	  DmPolicySchema,
6	  GroupPolicySchema,
7	  MarkdownConfigSchema,
8	  ReplyRuntimeConfigSchemaShape,
9	  ToolPolicySchema,
10	  requireOpenAllowFrom,
11	} from "openclaw/plugin-sdk/channel-config-schema";
12	import { z } from "openclaw/plugin-sdk/zod";
13	import { ircChannelConfigUiHints } from "./config-ui-hints.js";
14	
15	const IrcGroupSchema = z
16	  .object({
17	    requireMention: z.boolean().optional(),
18	    tools: ToolPolicySchema,
19	    toolsBySender: z.record(z.string(), ToolPolicySchema).optional(),
20	    skills: z.array(z.string()).optional(),
21	    enabled: z.boolean().optional(),
22	    allowFrom: z.array(z.union([z.string(), z.number()])).optional(),
23	    systemPrompt: z.string().optional(),
24	  })
25	  .strict();
26	
27	const IrcNickServSchema = z
28	  .object({
29	    enabled: z.boolean().optional(),
30	    service: z.string().optional(),
31	    password: z.string().optional(),
32	    passwordFile: z.string().optional(),
33	    register: z.boolean().optional(),
34	    registerEmail: z.string().optional(),
35	  })
36	  .strict()
37	  .superRefine((value, ctx) => {
38	    if (value.register && !value.registerEmail?.trim()) {
39	      ctx.addIssue({
40	        code: z.ZodIssueCode.custom,
41	        path: ["registerEmail"],
42	        message: "channels.irc.nickserv.register=true requires channels.irc.nickserv.registerEmail",
43	      });
44	    }
45	  });
46	
47	export const IrcAccountSchemaBase = z
48	  .object({
49	    name: z.string().optional(),
50	    enabled: z.boolean().optional(),
51	    dangerouslyAllowNameMatching: z.boolean().optional(),
52	    host: z.string().optional(),
53	    port: z.number().int().min(1).max(65535).optional(),
54	    tls: z.boolean().optional(),
55	    nick: z.string().optional(),
56	    username: z.string().optional(),
57	    realname: z.string().optional(),
58	    password: z.string().optional(),
59	    passwordFile: z.string().optional(),
60	    nickserv: IrcNickServSchema.optional(),
61	    dmPolicy: DmPolicySchema.optional().default("pairing"),
62	    allowFrom: z.array(z.union([z.string(), z.number()])).optional(),
63	    groupPolicy: GroupPolicySchema.optional().default("allowlist"),
64	    groupAllowFrom: z.array(z.union([z.string(), z.number()])).optional(),
65	    groups: z.record(z.string(), IrcGroupSchema.optional()).optional(),
66	    channels: z.array(z.string()).optional(),
67	    mentionPatterns: z.array(z.string()).optional(),
68	    markdown: MarkdownConfigSchema,
69	    ...ReplyRuntimeConfigSchemaShape,
70	  })
71	  .strict();
72	
73	export const IrcAccountSchema = IrcAccountSchemaBase.superRefine((value, ctx) => {
74	  requireOpenAllowFrom({
75	    policy: value.dmPolicy,
76	    allowFrom: value.allowFrom,
77	    ctx,
78	    path: ["allowFrom"],
79	    message: 'channels.irc.dmPolicy="open" requires channels.irc.allowFrom to include "*"',
80	  });
81	});
82	
83	export const IrcConfigSchema = IrcAccountSchemaBase.extend({
84	  accounts: z.record(z.string(), IrcAccountSchema.optional()).optional(),
85	  defaultAccount: z.string().optional(),
86	}).superRefine((value, ctx) => {
87	  requireOpenAllowFrom({
88	    policy: value.dmPolicy,
89	    allowFrom: value.allowFrom,
90	    ctx,
91	    path: ["allowFrom"],
92	    message: 'channels.irc.dmPolicy="open" requires channels.irc.allowFrom to include "*"',
93	  });
94	});
95	
96	export const IrcChannelConfigSchema = buildChannelConfigSchema(IrcConfigSchema, {
97	  uiHints: ircChannelConfigUiHints,
98	});

Perfect! I've successfully read all the files you requested. Here's the complete content organized by file:

---

## **Summary of IRC Plugin Architecture**

Based on the complete IRC plugin implementation, here are the key patterns for building a new channel plugin:

### **Core Entry Points**

1. **`index.ts`** - Main plugin export:
   - Uses `defineChannelPluginEntry()` with id, name, description
   - Exports the plugin instance and setRuntime function
   - Imports from `./src/channel.js`

2. **`setup-entry.ts`** - Setup wizard entry:
   - Uses `defineSetupPluginEntry(ircPlugin)`
   - Simple wrapper around the channel plugin

3. **`package.json`** - Plugin metadata:
   - Must have `openclaw.extensions`, `openclaw.setupEntry`, and `openclaw.channel` blocks
   - Specifies `minHostVersion`, entry points
   - Channel labels, icons, docs paths

### **Main Plugin Definition Pattern (`src/channel.ts`)**

The core pattern uses **`createChatChannelPlugin()`** with this structure:

```typescript
export const ircPlugin: ChannelPlugin<ResolvedAccount, ProbeType> = createChatChannelPlugin({
  base: {
    id: "irc",
    meta: { /* channel metadata */ },
    setup: setupAdapter,
    setupWizard: setupWizard,
    capabilities: {
      chatTypes: ["direct", "group"],
      media: true,
      blockStreaming: true,
    },
    reload: { configPrefixes: ["channels.irc"] },
    configSchema: IrcChannelConfigSchema,
    config: { /* config helpers */ },
    groups: { /* group policy resolvers */ },
    messaging: { /* target normalization */ },
    resolver: { /* target resolution */ },
    directory: { /* peer/group listing */ },
    status: { /* status and probing */ },
    gateway: { /* account startup */ },
  },
  pairing: { /* pairing text handler */ },
  security: { /* dm policy, warnings */ },
  outbound: { /* message sending */ },
});
```

### **Key Files Breakdown**

**`src/accounts.ts`** - Account resolution:
- Exports `ResolvedIrcAccount` type with enabled, configured, host, port, tls, nick, password fields
- `resolveIrcAccount()` - Merges config + environment variables
- `listEnabledIrcAccounts()` - Lists all enabled accounts

**`src/monitor.ts`** - Inbound message handling:
- `monitorIrcProvider()` - Connects and monitors for incoming messages
- `resolveIrcInboundTarget()` - Determines if message is group or DM
- Handles `onPrivmsg` events and passes to `handleIrcInbound()`

**`src/send.ts`** - Outbound messaging:
- `sendMessageIrc()` - Takes target and text, handles markdown tables
- Records activity via `getIrcRuntime().channel.activity.record()`

**`src/probe.ts`** - Health checking:
- `probeIrc()` - Tests connection, returns latency or error

**`src/normalize.ts`** - Data normalization:
- `normalizeIrcMessagingTarget()` - Handles "irc:", "channel:", "user:" prefixes
- `normalizeIrcAllowEntry()` - Normalizes allowlist entries
- `resolveIrcAllowlistMatch()` - Checks if sender matches allowlist

**`src/runtime.ts`** - Runtime storage:
- `createPluginRuntimeStore()` getter/setter for PluginRuntime instance
- `getIrcRuntime()`, `setIrcRuntime()`, `clearIrcRuntime()`

**`src/config-schema.ts`** - Zod schema:
- `IrcAccountSchemaBase` - Base account config shape
- `IrcConfigSchema` - Full config with accounts array
- `IrcChannelConfigSchema` - Final buildChannelConfigSchema wrapper

**`src/setup-core.ts`** - Setup adapter:
- `ircSetupAdapter: ChannelSetupAdapter` - Handles input validation and application
- Helper functions: `updateIrcAccountConfig()`, `setIrcDmPolicy()`, `setIrcAllowFrom()`, etc.

**`src/setup-surface.ts`** - Setup wizard UI:
- `ircSetupWizard: ChannelSetupWizard` - Defines all prompts, text inputs, group access, allowFrom section
- `promptIrcAllowFrom()` - Allowlist prompt helper
- `promptIrcNickServConfig()` - NickServ configuration

### **Configuration Structure**

Top-level under `channels.irc`:
- `enabled`, `name`, `host`, `port`, `tls`, `nick`, `username`, `realname`
- `password`, `passwordFile`, `nickserv` (service, password, passwordFile, register, registerEmail)
- `dmPolicy` ("pairing" or "open"), `allowFrom`, `groupPolicy` ("allowlist", "open", "disabled")
- `groups` (map of channel → config with requireMention, tools, allowFrom, etc.)
- `channels` (array of auto-join channels)
- `accounts` (map of additional account configs)
- `defaultAccount`, `dangerouslyAllowNameMatching`

### **Runtime Integration Pattern**

Accessed via `getIrcRuntime()` (PluginRuntime):
- `runtime.config.loadConfig()` - Get current config
- `runtime.channel.activity.record()` - Record inbound/outbound activity
- `runtime.logging.getChildLogger()` - Get logger instance

---

All files are now available for your reference. The pattern is highly structured with clear separation between:
1. **Account resolution** (resolve from config + environment)
2. **Messaging** (send/receive, normalization)
3. **Configuration** (schema, setup UI, adapter)
4. **Runtime** (plugin runtime storage, activity tracking)
5. **Plugin definition** (createChatChannelPlugin wrapper)

This should give you everything needed to build a new channel plugin following the same patterns!

input.summary:
Here's the implementation plan for the `extensions/pulseboard/` channel plugin.

## What gets created (22 new files, no modifications)

```
extensions/pulseboard/
├── openclaw.plugin.json      # manifest: id="pulseboard", channels=["pulseboard"]
├── package.json              # @openclaw/pulseboard, openclaw channel block
├── index.ts                  # defineChannelPluginEntry
├── setup-entry.ts            # defineSetupPluginEntry
├── api.ts                    # public barrel
├── runtime-api.ts            # PluginRuntime re-export
└── src/
    ├── types.ts              # ResolvedPulseBoardAccount + account helpers
    ├── config-schema.ts      # Zod schema (token, username, serverUrl, boards, dmPolicy)
    ├── config-ui-hints.ts    # UI label/placeholder hints
    ├── runtime.ts            # createPluginRuntimeStore → get/setPulseBoardRuntime
    ├── normalize.ts          # "#board" vs "@user" normalization, prefix stripping
    ├── send.ts               # sendMessagePulseBoard() via native fetch
    ├── probe.ts              # probePulseBoard() → GET /api/v1/me
    ├── monitor.ts            # monitorPulseBoardProvider() long-poll loop
    ├── session-route.ts      # resolvePulseBoardOutboundSessionRoute()
    ├── setup-core.ts         # pulseBoardSetupAdapter
    ├── setup-surface.ts      # pulseBoardSetupWizard (token, username, allowFrom prompts)
    ├── channel.ts            # createChatChannelPlugin — the main plugin
    ├── normalize.test.ts
    ├── types.test.ts
    ├── send.test.ts
    └── channel.test.ts
```

## Key design decisions

| Aspect | Choice |
|--------|--------|
| Chat types | `["direct", "group"]` — boards = groups, @username = direct |
| Text chunk limit | **4 000 chars**, `deliveryMode: "direct"` |
| Auth | Bearer token from env `PULSEBOARD_TOKEN` or config |
| Receive | Long-poll `GET /api/v1/updates?since=<ms>` |
| Send board | `POST /api/v1/boards/{boardId}/messages` |
| Send DM | `POST /api/v1/users/{userId}/messages` |
| External deps | None (uses native `fetch`) |
| Security | `createScopedDmSecurityResolver` + pairing flow |

All SDK imports go through `openclaw/plugin-sdk/*` — no core `src/**` imports.

## PulseBoard Channel Plugin — Plan Summary

**22 new files** in `extensions/pulseboard/` — **zero modifications** to any existing file.

### File Layout
```
extensions/pulseboard/
├── openclaw.plugin.json          # id="pulseboard", channels=["pulseboard"]
├── package.json                  # @openclaw/pulseboard, no external deps
├── index.ts                      # defineChannelPluginEntry
├── setup-entry.ts                # defineSetupPluginEntry
├── api.ts                        # public barrel
├── runtime-api.ts                # PluginRuntime re-export
└── src/
    ├── types.ts                  # ResolvedPulseBoardAccount + account helpers
    ├── config-schema.ts          # Zod: token, username, serverUrl, boards, dmPolicy, allowFrom
    ├── config-ui-hints.ts        # UI labels / placeholders
    ├── runtime.ts                # createPluginRuntimeStore
    ├── normalize.ts              # "#board" vs "@user" detection & normalization
    ├── send.ts                   # sendMessagePulseBoard() via native fetch
    ├── probe.ts                  # GET /api/v1/me health check
    ├── monitor.ts                # long-poll GET /api/v1/updates?since=<ms>
    ├── session-route.ts          # resolvePulseBoardOutboundSessionRoute()
    ├── setup-core.ts             # ChannelSetupAdapter
    ├── setup-surface.ts          # ChannelSetupWizard (token, username, allowFrom prompts)
    ├── channel.ts                # createChatChannelPlugin — main plugin
    ├── normalize.test.ts
    ├── types.test.ts
    ├── send.test.ts
    └── channel.test.ts
```

### Key Decisions
| Concern | Choice |
|---|---|
| Chat types | `["direct", "group"]` — `#board-name` = group, `@username` = DM |
| Text limit | **4 000 chars**, `deliveryMode: "direct"`, `chunkerMode: "text"` |
| Auth | Bearer token from `PULSEBOARD_TOKEN` env var or `config.token` |
| Receive | Long-poll `GET /api/v1/updates?since=<ms>` in `monitor.ts` |
| Send board | `POST /api/v1/boards/{boardId}/messages` |
| Send DM | `POST /api/v1/users/{userId}/messages` |
| Probe | `GET /api/v1/me` → `{ username, connected }` |
| Deps | None — plain `fetch`, no third-party packages |
| Security | `createScopedDmSecurityResolver` + standard pairing flow |
| Imports | All via `openclaw/plugin-sdk/*` only — no core `src/**` imports |

Here's the implementation plan for the `extensions/pulseboard/` channel plugin.

## What gets created (22 new files, no modifications)

```
extensions/pulseboard/
├── openclaw.plugin.json      # manifest: id="pulseboard", channels=["pulseboard"]
├── package.json              # @openclaw/pulseboard, openclaw channel block
├── index.ts                  # defineChannelPluginEntry
├── setup-entry.ts            # defineSetupPluginEntry
├── api.ts                    # public barrel
├── runtime-api.ts            # PluginRuntime re-export
└── src/
    ├── types.ts              # ResolvedPulseBoardAccount + account helpers
    ├── config-schema.ts      # Zod schema (token, username, serverUrl, boards, dmPolicy)
    ├── config-ui-hints.ts    # UI label/placeholder hints
    ├── runtime.ts            # createPluginRuntimeStore → get/setPulseBoardRuntime
    ├── normalize.ts          # "#board" vs "@user" normalization, prefix stripping
    ├── send.ts               # sendMessagePulseBoard() via native fetch
    ├── probe.ts              # probePulseBoard() → GET /api/v1/me
    ├── monitor.ts            # monitorPulseBoardProvider() long-poll loop
    ├── session-route.ts      # resolvePulseBoardOutboundSessionRoute()
    ├── setup-core.ts         # pulseBoardSetupAdapter
    ├── setup-surface.ts      # pulseBoardSetupWizard (token, username, allowFrom prompts)
    ├── channel.ts            # createChatChannelPlugin — the main plugin
    ├── normalize.test.ts
    ├── types.test.ts
    ├── send.test.ts
    └── channel.test.ts
```

## Key design decisions

| Aspect | Choice |
|--------|--------|
| Chat types | `["direct", "group"]` — boards = groups, @username = direct |
| Text chunk limit | **4 000 chars**, `deliveryMode: "direct"` |
| Auth | Bearer token from env `PULSEBOARD_TOKEN` or config |
| Receive | Long-poll `GET /api/v1/updates?since=<ms>` |
| Send board | `POST /api/v1/boards/{boardId}/messages` |
| Send DM | `POST /api/v1/users/{userId}/messages` |
| External deps | None (uses native `fetch`) |
| Security | `createScopedDmSecurityResolver` + pairing flow |

All SDK imports go through `openclaw/plugin-sdk/*` — no core `src/**` imports.

Here is the complete implementation plan for the PulseBoard channel plugin.

**22 new files** under `extensions/pulseboard/` — no existing files modified.

**Key design:**
- `chatTypes: ["direct", "group"]` — `#board-name` for boards (groups), `@username` for DMs
- `textChunkLimit: 4000`, `deliveryMode: "direct"`, `chunkerMode: "text"`
- Auth via Bearer token (`PULSEBOARD_TOKEN` env or config)
- Long-poll `GET /api/v1/updates?since=<ms>` for inbound messages
- `POST /api/v1/boards/{id}/messages` and `/api/v1/users/{id}/messages` for outbound
- `GET /api/v1/me` for health probe
- No external npm deps (uses native `fetch`)

**File layout** follows the Nostr/IRC pattern exactly:
- Root: `openclaw.plugin.json`, `package.json`, `index.ts`, `setup-entry.ts`, `api.ts`, `runtime-api.ts`
- `src/`: `types.ts`, `config-schema.ts`, `config-ui-hints.ts`, `runtime.ts`, `normalize.ts`, `send.ts`, `probe.ts`, `monitor.ts`, `session-route.ts`, `setup-core.ts`, `setup-surface.ts`, `channel.ts`
- Tests: `normalize.test.ts`, `types.test.ts`, `send.test.ts`, `channel.test.ts`

The plan file is at `/Users/timpietrusky/.claude/plans/swift-seeking-beacon.md` with full pseudocode for every file.

1	# Extensions Boundary
2	
3	This directory contains bundled plugins. Treat it as the same boundary that
4	third-party plugins see.
5	
6	## Public Contracts
7	
8	- Docs:
9	  - `docs/plugins/building-plugins.md`
10	  - `docs/plugins/architecture.md`
11	  - `docs/plugins/sdk-overview.md`
12	  - `docs/plugins/sdk-entrypoints.md`
13	  - `docs/plugins/sdk-runtime.md`
14	  - `docs/plugins/sdk-channel-plugins.md`
15	  - `docs/plugins/sdk-provider-plugins.md`
16	  - `docs/plugins/manifest.md`
17	- Definition files:
18	  - `src/plugin-sdk/plugin-entry.ts`
19	  - `src/plugin-sdk/core.ts`
20	  - `src/plugin-sdk/provider-entry.ts`
21	  - `src/plugin-sdk/channel-contract.ts`
22	  - `scripts/lib/plugin-sdk-entrypoints.json`
23	  - `package.json`
24	
25	## Boundary Rules
26	
27	- Extension production code should import from `openclaw/plugin-sdk/*` and its
28	  own local barrels such as `./api.ts` and `./runtime-api.ts`.
29	- Do not import core internals from `src/**`, `src/channels/**`,
30	  `src/plugin-sdk-internal/**`, or another extension's `src/**`.
31	- Do not use relative imports that escape the current extension package root.
32	- Keep plugin metadata accurate in `openclaw.plugin.json` and the package
33	  `openclaw` block so discovery and setup work without executing plugin code.
34	- Treat files like `src/**`, `onboard.ts`, and other local helpers as private
35	  unless you intentionally promote them through `api.ts` and, if needed, a
36	  matching `src/plugin-sdk/<id>.ts` facade.
37	- If core or core tests need a bundled plugin helper, export it from `api.ts`
38	  first instead of letting them deep-import extension internals.
39	
40	## Expanding The Boundary
41	
42	- If an extension needs a new seam, add a typed Plugin SDK subpath or additive
43	  export instead of reaching into core.
44	- Keep new plugin-facing seams backwards-compatible and versioned. Third-party
45	  plugins consume this surface.
46	- When intentionally expanding the contract, update the docs, exported subpath
47	  list, package exports, and API/contract checks in the same change.
48

1	import {
2	  buildChannelConfigSchema,
3	  BlockStreamingCoalesceSchema,
4	  DmConfigSchema,
5	  DmPolicySchema,
6	  GroupPolicySchema,
7	  MarkdownConfigSchema,
8	  ReplyRuntimeConfigSchemaShape,
9	  ToolPolicySchema,
10	  requireOpenAllowFrom,
11	} from "openclaw/plugin-sdk/channel-config-schema";
12	import { z } from "openclaw/plugin-sdk/zod";
13	import { ircChannelConfigUiHints } from "./config-ui-hints.js";
14	
15	const IrcGroupSchema = z
16	  .object({
17	    requireMention: z.boolean().optional(),
18	    tools: ToolPolicySchema,
19	    toolsBySender: z.record(z.string(), ToolPolicySchema).optional(),
20	    skills: z.array(z.string()).optional(),
21	    enabled: z.boolean().optional(),
22	    allowFrom: z.array(z.union([z.string(), z.number()])).optional(),
23	    systemPrompt: z.string().optional(),
24	  })
25	  .strict();
26	
27	const IrcNickServSchema = z
28	  .object({
29	    enabled: z.boolean().optional(),
30	    service: z.string().optional(),
31	    password: z.string().optional(),
32	    passwordFile: z.string().optional(),
33	    register: z.boolean().optional(),
34	    registerEmail: z.string().optional(),
35	  })
36	  .strict()
37	  .superRefine((value, ctx) => {
38	    if (value.register && !value.registerEmail?.trim()) {
39	      ctx.addIssue({
40	        code: z.ZodIssueCode.custom,
41	        path: ["registerEmail"],
42	        message: "channels.irc.nickserv.register=true requires channels.irc.nickserv.registerEmail",
43	      });
44	    }
45	  });
46	
47	export const IrcAccountSchemaBase = z
48	  .object({
49	    name: z.string().optional(),
50	    enabled: z.boolean().optional(),
51	    dangerouslyAllowNameMatching: z.boolean().optional(),
52	    host: z.string().optional(),
53	    port: z.number().int().min(1).max(65535).optional(),
54	    tls: z.boolean().optional(),
55	    nick: z.string().optional(),
56	    username: z.string().optional(),
57	    realname: z.string().optional(),
58	    password: z.string().optional(),
59	    passwordFile: z.string().optional(),
60	    nickserv: IrcNickServSchema.optional(),
61	    dmPolicy: DmPolicySchema.optional().default("pairing"),
62	    allowFrom: z.array(z.union([z.string(), z.number()])).optional(),
63	    groupPolicy: GroupPolicySchema.optional().default("allowlist"),
64	    groupAllowFrom: z.array(z.union([z.string(), z.number()])).optional(),
65	    groups: z.record(z.string(), IrcGroupSchema.optional()).optional(),
66	    channels: z.array(z.string()).optional(),
67	    mentionPatterns: z.array(z.string()).optional(),
68	    markdown: MarkdownConfigSchema,
69	    ...ReplyRuntimeConfigSchemaShape,
70	  })
71	  .strict();
72	
73	export const IrcAccountSchema = IrcAccountSchemaBase.superRefine((value, ctx) => {
74	  requireOpenAllowFrom({
75	    policy: value.dmPolicy,
76	    allowFrom: value.allowFrom,
77	    ctx,
78	    path: ["allowFrom"],
79	    message: 'channels.irc.dmPolicy="open" requires channels.irc.allowFrom to include "*"',
80	  });
81	});
82	
83	export const IrcConfigSchema = IrcAccountSchemaBase.extend({
84	  accounts: z.record(z.string(), IrcAccountSchema.optional()).optional(),
85	  defaultAccount: z.string().optional(),
86	}).superRefine((value, ctx) => {
87	  requireOpenAllowFrom({
88	    policy: value.dmPolicy,
89	    allowFrom: value.allowFrom,
90	    ctx,
91	    path: ["allowFrom"],
92	    message: 'channels.irc.dmPolicy="open" requires channels.irc.allowFrom to include "*"',
93	  });
94	});
95	
96	export const IrcChannelConfigSchema = buildChannelConfigSchema(IrcConfigSchema, {
97	  uiHints: ircChannelConfigUiHints,
98	});
99

38	| Concern | Choice |
39	|---|---|
40	| Chat types | `["direct", "group"]` — `#board-name` = group, `@username` = DM |
41	| Text limit | **4 000 chars**, `deliveryMode: "direct"`, `chunkerMode: "text"` |
42	| Auth | Bearer token from `PULSEBOARD_TOKEN` env var or `config.token` |
43	| Receive | Long-poll `GET /api/v1/updates?since=<ms>` in `monitor.ts` |
44	| Send board | `POST /api/v1/boards/{boardId}/messages` |
45	| Send DM | `POST /api/v1/users/{userId}/messages` |
46	| Probe | `GET /api/v1/me` → `{ username, connected }` |
47	| Deps | None — plain `fetch`, no third-party packages |
48	| Security | `createScopedDmSecurityResolver` + standard pairing flow |

The plan is complete and saved. Here's a summary for your review:

---

## PulseBoard Channel Plugin — Plan Summary

**22 new files** in `extensions/pulseboard/` — **zero modifications** to any existing file.

### File Layout
```
extensions/pulseboard/
├── openclaw.plugin.json          # id="pulseboard", channels=["pulseboard"]
├── package.json                  # @openclaw/pulseboard, no external deps
├── index.ts                      # defineChannelPluginEntry
├── setup-entry.ts                # defineSetupPluginEntry
├── api.ts                        # public barrel
├── runtime-api.ts                # PluginRuntime re-export
└── src/
    ├── types.ts                  # ResolvedPulseBoardAccount + account helpers
    ├── config-schema.ts          # Zod: token, username, serverUrl, boards, dmPolicy, allowFrom
    ├── config-ui-hints.ts        # UI labels / placeholders
    ├── runtime.ts                # createPluginRuntimeStore
    ├── normalize.ts              # "#board" vs "@user" detection & normalization
    ├── send.ts                   # sendMessagePulseBoard() via native fetch
    ├── probe.ts                  # GET /api/v1/me health check
    ├── monitor.ts                # long-poll GET /api/v1/updates?since=<ms>
    ├── session-route.ts          # resolvePulseBoardOutboundSessionRoute()
    ├── setup-core.ts             # ChannelSetupAdapter
    ├── setup-surface.ts          # ChannelSetupWizard (token, username, allowFrom prompts)
    ├── channel.ts                # createChatChannelPlugin — main plugin
    ├── normalize.test.ts
    ├── types.test.ts
    ├── send.test.ts
    └── channel.test.ts
```

### Key Decisions
| Concern | Choice |
|---|---|
| Chat types | `["direct", "group"]` — `#board-name` = group, `@username` = DM |
| Text limit | **4 000 chars**, `deliveryMode: "direct"`, `chunkerMode: "text"` |
| Auth | Bearer token from `PULSEBOARD_TOKEN` env var or `config.token` |
| Receive | Long-poll `GET /api/v1/updates?since=<ms>` in `monitor.ts` |
| Send board | `POST /api/v1/boards/{boardId}/messages` |
| Send DM | `POST /api/v1/users/{userId}/messages` |
| Probe | `GET /api/v1/me` → `{ username, connected }` |
| Deps | None — plain `fetch`, no third-party packages |
| Security | `createScopedDmSecurityResolver` + standard pairing flow |
| Imports | All via `openclaw/plugin-sdk/*` only — no core `src/**` imports |
